Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To His Requests For Production Of Documents, Set Two, From Forward Air
Off Calendar Oct-07-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Plaintiff's Motion to Compel Further Responses to His Requests for Production of Documents, Set Two, From Forward Air, Inc. is GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART.

On 3/3/25, plaintiff e-served the requests for production ("RFP") on Forward Air, Inc. (Jollie Decl., Ex. A.) On 5/5/25, "Forward Air, Inc." e-served an untimely response which was composed of waived and spurious objections. (Id. at Ex. C.)

In any event, the requests at issue are reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. (CCP Section 2017.010.) Defendant failed to provide timely responses, then the late responses were wholly inadequate. Most of Forward Air, Inc.'s arguments lack merit. It claims that it served supplemental responses prior to the hearing. But those responses are on behalf of "Sunshine Transportation." There is apparently some relationship between Forward Air, Inc. and Sunshine Transportation, but there is no competent evidence explaining it. Forward Air Inc. therefore must provide additional responses, especially since "Forward Air, Inc." provided the original response.

The court orders Forward Air, Inc. to provide further verified responses to the RFP within 10 days of notice of entry of this order. Forward Air, Inc. waived its objections.

Forward Air Inc.'s concerns regarding the "Reptile Theory" and undue prejudice at trial are a matter for the trial judge.

Plaintiff's request for sanctions is DENIED since he failed to comply with the mandatory requirements of Code of Civil Procedure section 2023.040 [sanctions request in notice of motion].)

Sanctions are, nevertheless, mandatory because the court does not find that Defendant actions in connection with this discovery were substantially justified nor does it find that imposition of sanctions would otherwise be unjust. (See Code of Civil Procedure section 2031.310(h).) Defendant Forward Air, Inc. shall pay $500 as sanctions, payable to the Clerk of the Court, payment no later than October 31, 2025, receipt filed no later than November 5, 2025. Such payment reimburses the court for a portion of the costs spent adjudicating this motion.

Order is signed.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn; Clerk: M. Macadangdang; Not Reported. (302/JMQ)

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-10-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Corey Jollie, Esq., COOPERS LLP 415-434-2111 Facsimile: 415-434-2112 corey@coopers.law Attorney for Plaintiff's BRIAN MACGREGOR and CAREY SUCKOW
Davis J. Reilly, Esq., BLEDSOE, DIESTEL, TREPPA & CRANE (415) 981-5411 dreilly@bledsoelaw.com Attorneys for Defendant PRIORITY 1, INC., doing business in California as PRIORITY-1 LOGISTICS (via ZOOM)

hearing held. Discussed, not argued. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT PRIORITY 1, INC., DOING BUSINESS IN CALIFORNIA AS PRIORITY-1 LOGISTICS DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT

Defendant Priority 1, Inc.'s demurrer to all four causes of action alleged in the first amended complaint (FAC) filed by plaintiffs Brian MacGregor and Carey Sukow is overruled as to all four causes of action. The sole grounds for the demurrer is that all four claims against Priority are preempted by the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act because those claims are based on Priority's broker services. The FAC does not allege that Priority is sued because of broker services it performed. Rather, paragraph 8 of the FAC alleges that Priority "organized, coordinated and/or participated" with others in "the transportation of the Subject Bar." Priority seeks to fill this missing allegation by requesting judicial notice that it is a property broker licensed by the United States Department of Transportation and that the "only reasonable interpretation " is "that Plaintiffs' claims against Priority-1 are predicated on its apparent selection of a motor carrier to transport freight." Priority cites no authority, nor is the court aware of any, that on a demurrer a California trial court may engage in a "reasonable interpretation" analysis of alleged facts in a pleading, as opposed to accepting as true the facts as alleged and liberally construing them in favor of the pleading party. Because Priority's broker license does not establish as a matter of law that the claims against it are based on broker services, its preemption argument fails at this stage of the case.

Order is signed.

Judge: Harold E. Kahn; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/HEK)

DEMURRER to CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-29-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: CROSS-DEFENDANTS FORWARD AIR, INC., AND KRISNIL PRASAD'S DEMURRER TO CROSS-COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Cross-defendants Forward Air, Inc. and Krisnil Prasad's demurrer is sustained with leave to amend.

In June 2023, Brian McGregor filed a complaint against several parties, including Street Factory Media, Inc., Forward Air, Inc. and Krisnil Prasad, seeking damages for injuries he suffered when a crate holding a 1,000 pound portable bar fell off a truck and onto MacGregor. MacGregor's spouse is also a plaintiff, on a claim for lost wages due to providing care for MacGregor. MacGregor alleges Street Factory Media built the bar and the shipping crate, "prepared to ship [the bar]," and contracted with Forward Air for the shipping. Forward Air shipped the bar in the crate from Minnesota to San Francisco. Prasad operated the truck in which the bar and crate were shipped. In September 2023, Street Factory Media filed a cross-complaint against Forward Air and Prasad that included a claim for negligence, the second cause of action. Forward Air and Prasad filed a demurrer arguing Street Factory Media failed to state a cause of action upon which relief can be granted.

The issue on demurrer is, taking the facts properly pleaded and properly noticed as true, does the challenged cause of action necessarily fail to state a cause for relief. (See Code Civ. Proc., Sec. 430.10(e); Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318.) In assessing whether the complaint states a cause of action, the court accepts all properly pleaded material facts, but not contentions, deductions, or conclusions of fact or law. (Minton v. Dignity Health (2019), 39 Cal.App.5th 1155, 1161.) "[I]f, on consideration of all facts stated, it appears that plaintiff is entitled to any relief against defendant, the complaint will be held good, though facts may not be clearly stated, or may be intermingled with statement of other facts irrelevant to cause of action shown, or though plaintiff may demand relief to which he is not entitled under facts alleged." (Augustine v. Trucco (1954) 124 Cal.App.2d 229, 236, quoting Matteson v. Wagoner (1905) 147 Cal. 739, 742.)

Here, Street Factory Media's second cause of action fails to state a negligence claim against Forward Air and Prasad. For example, Street Factory Media has not adequately alleged duty and breach. A breach of "contractual duties" does not, by itself, sound in tort or negligence. The same for a breach of duties "based on agreements." As a general matter, everyone must exercise reasonable care to avoid injuring another (see Civ. Code, Sec. 1714, subd. (a)), but Street Factory Media does not allege any facts showing that either Forward Air or Prasad breached the reasonable-care duty they owed to Street Factory Media. Merely stating the legal conclusion is not sufficient. Street Factory Media's "fiduciary duty" allegation is insufficient because it, too, is a legal conclusion without facts-even ultimate facts-that, if proved, would support a finding that either Forward Air or Prasad assumed a fiduciary duty towards Street Factory Media and breached that duty; a contract does not necessarily (or even generally) create a fiduciary duty. Contrary to Forward Air and Prasad's contention, it is not true that litigation expenses categorically are excluded from the scope of damages recoverable in negligence. However, absent more information regarding the duty and breach, the court cannot say whether such expenses are within the scope of damages recoverable by Street Factory Media under a negligence theory in this action.

The court grants Street Factory Media's request for leave to amend. Street Factory Media shall have 15 days from notice of entry of this order to file a first amended cross-complaint.

Order signed.
